In theory I would support a Basic Income.  I am skeptical that costs would be contained.  One basic premise is that in order to make it cost effective, the current social welfare administrative bureaucracy would be made redundant (i.e. no more prying social workers making sure you are "deserving" of public money).  Others have mentioned there would be less recourse to first responders as well.  Does anyone really believe government will restructure itself appropriately and accordingly?  I believe at least one public sector union has already spoken out against Basic Income - would their members still be guaranteed a job (for life) if they were no longer needed to poke around someone else's life?  Would the police/firefighters union agree to review how many of their members are truly needed?  I doubt it.

In 1979 I was working for a logging company.  Back then our forests provided jobs for our communities and funded our public schools along with many other things that are now funded by tax bonds or subsidized by federal dollars.  Logging companies and their employees were volunteer first responders to forest fires in their area.  After all, they had a vested interest in maintaining a healthy forest.  Plus they had dozers and water trucks already in the area.  I never had to fight fire because we didn't have any.  Logging was managed to create fire breaks and the spur roads were maintained.  Wildlife flourished back then and there were game trails everywhere.  I recently spent about 3 weeks driving the logging roads in the high cascades of Linn, Marion and Lane counties.  No game trails, spur roads blocked, grown over or gated and rare human activity.  I don't believe that our new ways are working out.

Of course judges interpret laws, but they also make law and clarify law.  

In 9 provinces and 3 territories of Canada, we live in a Common Law regime.  That means, by definition, judge-made law.  Of course there is also legislation, but it doesn't mean judges don't still make law. They do.  They've been doing it for almost a thousand years, and are still doing it.

You may be surprised to find out that during three years at law school, students spend little time reading legislation, but many hours reading case law: judicial decisions in which judges have made law and sometimes (we hope) clarified it.

In the case under discussion, the Charter is involved to aid judges in making law, but there will be no legislation overturned or invalidated.  That isn't what it's about.

By the way, judges are still engaged in clarifying the law in Quebec's civil law system, but that's a different story.

The reasonable assumption, which was upheld on appeal, is that the jury acquitted the defendant because the accuser's testimony wasn't truthful. The appeal noted that the use of some evidence at trial shouldn't have been allowed, but that the accuser's false testimony at trial was sufficient for the jury to come to a not guilty verdict anyway.

Yes, it's certainly the case that a woman's sexual history may not be pertinent at trial. But the far more important point is that a man should not be convicted of sexual assault based on a woman's false testimony.

The best and most recent review I have read of Billing and Insurance Related costs (BIR)  (Aliya Jiwani, et al) concludes that such costs for care from paid for by public and private providers are about 14.7% of total costs.  That same study etimates that about 49% of the 14.7% is paid by private insurers.  The Kaiser Family Foundation says that about 56% of us have private health care insurance.  Someone is paying a disproportionate share of these costs.   A Heritage Foundation study concluded that Medicare patients cost about 10% more for administrative (BIR) costs than did privately insured patients.  The fact is that these are all estimates and some may be influenced by political agendas.  My personal experience in the VA versus a private non-profit clinic where I worked for five years, is that we did more reporting and other administrative functions at the VA.  The $20-bandage and other similar absurd prices are part of cost shifting schemes to pay for uninsured patients.

Conclusion - by far most of Ontario's growth is channeled into the GTA. In fact, if the Ottawa and Kitchener-Waterloo areas are taken out of the equation, Ontario's population probably shrank.
Which suggests as part of long term planning, the provincial and federal governments should offer encouragements to growth in other parts of Ontario beyond the GTA.

Have you tried rebounding exercise? 

My arthritis was so bad that I couldn't use a stationary bike or even walk very far without intense hip and knee pain. Pain was stopping me from all forms of exercise. Out of desperation, I bought a rebounder - the kind with bungee cords versus springs. I have greatly reduced the level of arthritic pain to almost zero, without OTC meds like ibuprofen or ASA. You know how massage is painful for tight muscles but then you feel relief because it stimulates blood circulation? Rebounding on a quality rebounder is like a full body massage - it improves lymphatic and blood circulation, tissue oxygenation, metabolism, release of endorphins, etc. It's weight-bearing, cardiovascular exercise without impact - it's fun as well. You have to ease into it starting with a "health bounce" and gradually increase to jumping and other exercises. Aqua-aerobics is another good alternative.

I wish people would get off this "dump the two party system" kick.  You will always have a two party system, regardless of what you call it, a minority and a majority party.

If you offer dozens of parties to chose from people will find a candidate that more closely resembles your values, but will not garner the support of a majority of voters.  In a multiparty system no one party will have the majority. They must form a coalition, with a coalition platform that is a compromise of all of the parties that come together to form a major and minority coalition.  

In the US, we skip that part and form our coalition now.  You will be no better served by multiple parties. You'll just be happy when you vote, and unhappy when the form a coalition and you find your values diluted.

As much as you hate it, as much as  you think it is hypocritical it will ALWAYs be a two party system.  Unless you are so radical your values are off the chart, you'll rarely be given two equally repellent choices.

I'm covered by both the VA and Medicare; never used the Medicare.  But we put our daughter on an HSA until ObamaCare disqualified it.  An HSA isn't insurance.  It's a savings account to pay for "routine maintenance."  It has to be coupled with a separate catastrophic major medical policy with a large deductible - typically $10,000, but you can raise the deductible to lower your premiums as your HSA account builds up - to cover the large, unanticipated and catastrophic, but rare claims.  Before Obamacare came along, they were cheap.  Most HSA administrators allow the money to be invested in mutual funds or safer savings accounts, and issue you a debit card.  I'd add a credit card to cover the deductible if you have a large claim before you've had a chance to build up your HSA, which works like an IRA if you don't spend the money by staying healthy and not running up unnecessary costs.  Most of us don't run up $3,000/yr in costs every year.  Rand Paul's plan lets you put in more.

These are State projects, not "Federal" projects! US Treasury money is not the funding source for highway and airport improvements. Rather, both are normally funded by dedicated trust funds, which are comprised of taxes paid for by local airport and highway users. In other words, Alaskans as passengers, shippers and aircraft operators pay into a fund which is utilized for said improvements. In the case of airports, the FAA administers those "grants" through a complicated series of rules and design standards. Airport operators receive these funds based upon a formula need basis for which they pay matching funds to get. That is why each year the Legislature gives the Capital Budget high priority, in that these trust funds account for anywhere from 80% to 95% of all capital projects. Without appropriation of Alaska State matching funds the trust fund monies go to other projects in other States.

One solution of labor cost avoidance of large businesses is for the State legislature to adopt a "Public Assistance Service Surcharge" (PASS) Recovery Tax.  Businesses would avoid this PASS Recovery Tax in two ways,  1. pay a living wage that raises employees above the threshold for Public Assistance eligibility; and / or 2. provide employees a 40 hour full time position at a living wage.  If a businesses chooses to do neither, the PASS Recovery Tax on the business would be the aggregate gross amount of Public Assistance benefits for the business's employee(s) paid by the government (taxpayers), plus a 3% interest charge on the gross amount.  Or, if the business chooses to pay a living wage and sufficient hours for its employees, and thus not eligible for Public Assistance, then the business would receive (as is the cost deduction is anyway) a 100% cost of business deduction for labor costs, plus a 5% tax credit applied to business's tax debt. Small things like scratched woodwork, grimy areas on paint or around doorknobs and switchplates, cobwebs in ceiling corners, sagging drapes and blinds, mineral-scale buildup around water fixtures, and wobbly hardware on kitchen cabinets can cause a potential buyer to decide to look elsewhere or make a low offer. I have traveled through almost every state in the country and several foreign countries and have never seen a worse situation for red light running than right here in Hawaii.  Green lights turn to yellow for several seconds to allow approaching vehicles time to slow down and stop before the light turns red, but that is not the norm in Hawaii.  What happens here is that most drivers speed up when light turn yellow to “beat the light” before turning red, which usually results in vehicles entering and passing through intersections after lights have turned to red.    Since it is so normal, drivers waiting at traffic lights rarely jump red lights in anticipation of them turning green, a common practice on the mainland, for fear of being run into by a red-light runner.

Foam insulation is also very common behind interior walls in Canada, but the drywall increases the time bfor a fire breaks through to it.

The core issue is the sustainability of the dividends and EIF's use of FCF less maintenance capex (but not growth capex) as the denominator in their payout ratio calculation.

In Q2 and H1 2017 EIF reports payout ratios of 75% and 115% respectively. 
In the same periods their FCF - maintenance capex was $21.8 million and $28.2 million respectively. However, growth capex was $33.1 million and $98.8 million respectively. So if you take that into account a 'hard' measure of FCF would be negative. That's the nub of the concern.

"Invasive species" refers exclusively to those deliberately or inadvertently introduced outside their natural ranges via human agency.

The problem with this is that naturally-occurring biota evolve together over thousands or millions of years, struggling to eat and not be eaten, to where said biota establishes mutually sustainable numbers (which usually fluctuate cyclically).  In a stable system like this the prey don't overpopulate and eliminate their food supply and starve, and the predators don't overpopulate, eradicate their prey and then starve (or succumb to disease outbreaks).

Introduction of invasive species destabilizes such systems.  The more invasives introduced, the longer the system takes to restabilize and the more native species become extinct.  Research chestnut blight.

What is a close familial relationship for the purposes of determining if someone is subject to the E.O.? 

In light of the July 13, 2017 U.S. District Court of Hawaii ruling regarding the definition of “close familial relationship” as that phrase was used in the Supreme Court’s June 26, 2017 order on implementing Section 2(c) of E.O. 13780, a close familial relationship is defined as a parent (including parent-in-law), spouse, fiancé, child, adult son or daughter, son-in-law, daughter-in-law, sibling, grandparents, grandchildren, brothers-in-law, sisters-in-law, aunts and uncles, ‎nephews and nieces, and cousins. For this purpose, “cousins” are limited to first-cousins (i.e., each cousin has a parent who is a sibling of a parent of the other cousin). For all relationships, half or step status is included (e.g., “half-brother” or “step-sister”). “Close familial relations” does not include any other “extended” family members, such as second-cousins. - Travel.State.Gov alert, 14 Jul 2017

I rented a moving van late one November and drove it from Vancouver to Winnipeg, just barely staying ahead of a snowstorm.  The thing is, the van had summer weight washer fluid that froze before we even left Salmon Arm.  So here I was driving through the mountains at night, oncoming traffic spraying dirty mist all over, and I couldn't clean the windshield...  Nowhere to pull over...  Washer fluid nozzles frozen solid and no way to thaw them...

I will never, never do that again!  I'm lucky I didn't drive over a cliff or drive head-on into somebody else.

The truck had an Arizona fleet licence plate but I rented it in Canada for a trip to Winnipeg, and it still came with summer washer fluid.  I can try to pass blame onto the rental staff for not changing the fluid, but that's not fair.  I was the driver and it was ultimately my responsibility. All I can say is: be sure to check things (like washer fluid) for yourself and never take things like that for granted.
